title: The Fed Tightening Cycle: JPMorgan Chase
setupNarrative:
It's March 14, 2022. JPMorgan Chase is trading at $135.78, and the Federal Reserve's FOMC meeting begins tomorrow, March 15-16. After months of insisting inflation was "transitory," the Fed has pivoted hawkishly. CPI has surged to 7.9%, the highest since 1982, and Fed Chair Jerome Powell has all but promised the first rate hike since 2018. The CME FedWatch tool shows a 95% probability of a 25 basis point hike, with markets pricing in six to seven total hikes in 2022.

Conventional wisdom says banks benefit from rising rates because it widens their net interest margins -- the spread between what they earn on loans and pay on deposits. JPMorgan's net interest income was $52.3 billion in 2021, and management guided for higher NII as rates rise. The KBW Bank Index has outperformed the S&P 500 by 8 percentage points year-to-date on this thesis. JPM's own Q4 earnings beat expectations, and CEO Jamie Dimon expressed optimism about the rate environment.

However, the picture is more nuanced than "rates up = banks up." Rising rates also increase recession risk, which drives up loan losses. The yield curve is flattening dangerously, with the 2-10 spread at just 25 basis points -- a curve inversion would be a recession warning signal that historically hammers bank stocks. Meanwhile, Russia's invasion of Ukraine on February 24 has injected massive uncertainty into global markets. JPMorgan disclosed $1 billion in Russia exposure. Options IV sits at 34%, and the market is torn between the bullish rate narrative and growing macro risks.

headlines: Fed set to raise rates for first time since 2018 amid 7.9% inflation
Banks rally on rising rate expectations; JPMorgan seen as prime beneficiary
Yield curve flattens to 25bps as recession fears grow alongside rate hikes
JPMorgan discloses $1 billion Russia exposure as Ukraine crisis deepens

revealNarrative:
The Fed delivered the expected 25 basis point rate hike on March 16, and initially, the "banks benefit from rising rates" thesis played out. JPMorgan rallied to $143 within a week as the market digested the Fed's dot plot showing aggressive tightening ahead. But the rally proved short-lived.

By late April, reality set in. The yield curve briefly inverted on April 1 (2-year yields exceeded 10-year yields), flashing a recession warning. JPMorgan's Q1 2022 earnings on April 13 showed a $902 million reserve build for expected loan losses -- a reversal from the reserve releases that had boosted 2021 profits. The stock plunged 3.2% on the report. CEO Dimon warned of "significant" geopolitical and economic headwinds. By June 17, JPM had fallen to $115 -- a 15.3% decline from our entry point, despite (or because of) the Fed hiking rates at the most aggressive pace in decades.

The optimal strategy was a straddle or strangle, capturing the initial rally and the subsequent decline. The $145 April calls gained 85% in the first week as JPM rallied, while the $120 June puts gained over 40% by expiration. Those who simply bought calls on the "rising rates = bullish banks" thesis were caught in a trap. The lesson: in complex macro environments, the second-order effects (recession risk, loan losses, curve flattening) can overwhelm the first-order thesis (wider net interest margins).

outcomeData.explanation: JPMorgan's price action around the Fed rate hike cycle illustrates the complexity of macro trades. The stock initially rallied 5% on rate hike optimism, then reversed and fell 15% over three months as recession fears, yield curve inversion, and loan loss reserves overwhelmed the net interest margin thesis. A straddle captured both moves, with the short-term call leg profiting first, then the longer-dated put leg paying off. This teaches that consensus macro narratives ('rates up = banks up') often oversimplify, and straddles are valuable when the direction is uncertain but a big move is likely.
